game_name: Dungeons & Dragons: Eye of the Beholder

design_philosophy.core_loop: First-person dungeon crawling with turn-based party combat, resource management, and puzzle-solving. Navigate multi-level dungeons, recruit NPCs, engage in tactical combat, manage inventory and spells.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (mana, health, supplies) against escalating enemy encounters. Unforgiving dungeon design creates constant threat of party wipe. Exploration of unknown territory with irreversible decisions (character death, permanent NPC loss).
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Leading a custom adventuring party through dangerous dungeons as a tactical commander. Building a roster through NPC recruitment. Experiencing tabletop D&D in first-person dungeon exploration format.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unforgiving difficulty philosophy paired with faithful AD&D rule implementation. Emphasis on puzzle-solving over pure combat—a refreshing deviation from typical dungeon crawlers. No hand-holding; player must manage party composition, spell selection, and resource allocation with minimal guidance. Abrupt, minimal ending reflects commitment to dungeon experience over narrative spectacle.

difficulty.lives_system: Permadeath for party members unless resurrected or replaced. Character death is permanent failure state unless player has revival resources.
difficulty.checkpointing: No checkpointing system. Progress saved between dungeon levels, but death mid-dungeon forces reload. Unforgiving progression structure.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through party composition customization, spell selection for casters, NPC recruitment strategy, and item management. Players can adjust challenge through roster decisions and preparation, but dungeon encounters remain uncompromising.

progression.upgrade_system: Character experience and leveling. Spell acquisition through discovery and NPC teaching. Equipment upgrades found in dungeons. Party size expansion from 4 to maximum 6 through NPC recruitment or skeleton resurrection.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression blocked by dungeon level structure. Access to deeper areas requires defeating or bypassing obstacles in previous levels. NPC recruitment expands party capability. Spells unlocked through character advancement and NPC interactions.
replayability_hooks: Party import system allows carrying roster through sequels (Eye of the Beholder II and III)
Multiple NPC recruitment paths and roster combinations
Puzzle solutions may vary based on party composition and available spells
Permadeath creates unique narratives per playthrough

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through dungeon architecture (sewer → dwarf clans → drow clans → beholder lair progression)
NPC dialogue revealing backstory and dungeon lore
Tabletop D&D setting (Waterdeep, Xanathar) provides established world context
Minimal cutscenes; story emerges from exploration and NPC interaction rather than exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Climactic encounter serving as narrative payoff to dungeon descent. Xanathar positioned as final obstacle after multi-tier progression through hostile territories. Boss encounter rewards completion with minimal narrative closure (text description of return to surface, heroes welcome).
notable_bosses: Xanathar (beholder) - final boss, eponymous antagonist blocking initial sewer entrance and lurking in deepest lair

faction_indicators: NPC recruits with individual allegiances and backgrounds
danger_signals: Xanathar's eye-based magic and beholder capabilities
Escalating monster encounters per dungeon level
Permanently blocked sewer entrance (Xanathar's collapse) creating one-way descent
Permadeath mechanics signal unforgiving threat level
environmental_storytelling: Sewer architecture establishing underground city infrastructure
Dwarf clan territory showing organized civilization in depths
Drow settlements revealing rival underground cultures
Xanathar's lair as final antagonist space reflecting beholder's power and dominion
Corpses and ruins of fallen adventurers throughout dungeons

sources_used: wikipedia - Eye of the Beholder (video game)
